headline: Four navies, one tactic: law enforcement as coercion at sea
deck: In one week, Iran, China, the US and the EU all reached for the same move: stop a ship, demand its papers, call it law enforcement. None of them called it war. That's the trick — and it isn't going away.
body: Read the week's shipping news as one story and a pattern jumps out. Iran's Revolutionary Guard, in the EU's own words, "assumed control of the Strait of Hormuz," set up a toll, and demanded papers before ships were "allowed to transit." [E1] China's transport ministry ran a "special maritime traffic law enforcement operation" east of Taiwan and said it inspected 198 passing ships to "exercise China's maritime administrative law enforcement jurisdiction." [E2] Taiwan's foreign ministry called it exactly that — China "illegally harassed cargo ships exercising their right to freedom of navigation in waters surrounding Taiwan," it said. [E3] Nobody called any of it war. They called it administration.

That word is the whole trick, and it breaks the law. Hormuz is an international strait, and the rule is blunt: the right to pass through one "shall not be impeded" and can't be switched off. [E4] A country can inspect ships in narrow cases — illegal fishing in its own waters, a suspected pirate — but it can't turn a strait into a tollbooth where passage is a favour it grants. The new move isn't the inspection. It's the relabeling: an international waterway treated as a national checkpoint.

Each one gets there by a different route. Iran mixes safety rules, wartime-closure talk, and domestic security; one legal analysis points out that closing Hormuz isn't really a blockade, since a blockade targets an enemy and Hormuz traffic is mostly neutral. [E5] China dresses a sovereignty claim up as routine traffic cops. The US, blowing the engines off blockade-running tankers, is using wartime powers in the language of "compliance." The EU reached for sanctions. Four different rulebooks, one behaviour.

And that's why it's a method, not a moment. An inspection is cheaper than a shot and easier to deny. It dumps the risk onto the ship's captain. And transit by transit, it makes normal a claim the law says can't be made. Watch the path it runs: papers, then a warning shot, then a hull. Off Sirik on 12 June, that's exactly the sequence that played out — while a peace deal was supposedly days away.

evidence_box.fragment: The IRGC Navy "assumed control of the Strait of Hormuz," "implemented a toll system," and required papers before vessels were "allowed to transit."
A "special maritime traffic law enforcement operation" east of Taiwan inspected 198 passing vessels to "exercise China's maritime administrative law enforcement jurisdiction."
Taiwan's MOFA: China "illegally harassed cargo ships exercising their right to freedom of navigation in waters surrounding Taiwan," "infringing upon Taiwan's sovereign rights."
UNCLOS Articles 37–44 govern transit passage through international straits; transit passage "shall not be impeded" and is not suspendable.
Alexander Lott argues a closure of Hormuz is often mislabeled a blockade: a blockade targets an enemy coast, while Hormuz closure affects neutral shipping through an international strait.
